---
title: "Lesson 3: One-Sample Confidence Intervals Based on the t-Distribution"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---


::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_t-distribution-confidence-intervals.mp4 >}}

[Transcript](01_t-distribution-confidence-intervals.en.txt)
[Handout](02_video-slides-for-t-distribution-confidence-intervals_module4_lesson3.pdf){width=700 height=980}
:::

## Slides on Large-Sample Confidence Intervals with Unknown Variance {#sec-large-sample-confidence-intervals}

Suppose that $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$ is a random sample from a distribution with

$$
\mathbb{E}[X_i]=\mu
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\operatorname{Var}(X_i)=\sigma^2
$$

Assume that

- $n$ is large, using $n>30$ as a rule of thumb
- both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are unknown

By the Central Limit Theorem (CLT), the sample mean is approximately normally distributed:

$$
\overline{X}
\overset{\mathrm{asymp}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

Equivalently,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
&\xrightarrow{d}
\mathcal{N}(0,1) \\
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
&\overset{\mathrm{approx}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(0,1)
&& \text{for large }n
\end{aligned}
$$

To construct an approximate $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$, begin with

$$
-z_{1-\alpha/2}
<
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
<
z_{1-\alpha/2}
$$

Solving for $\mu$ would give

$$
\overline{X}
\pm
z_{1-\alpha/2}\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
$$

but $\sigma$ is unknown

## Sample Variance {#sec-sample-variance}

::: {#def-sample-variance}
## Sample Variance

The sample variance is

$$
S^2
=
\frac{1}{n-1}
\sum_{i=1}^{n}
\left(X_i-\overline{X}\right)^2
$$

An equivalent computational formula is

$$
\begin{aligned}
S^2
&=
\frac{
\displaystyle \sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i^2
-
\displaystyle \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n}X_i\right)^2}{n}
}{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$
:::

The sample variance is consistent for the population variance:

$$
S^2 \xrightarrow{P} \sigma^2
$$

Thus, for large $n$, $S^2$ is close to $\sigma^2$ and $S$ can replace $\sigma$ in the standardized sample mean:

$$
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}
\overset{\mathrm{approx}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(0,1)
$$

Solving

$$
-z_{1-\alpha/2}
<
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}
<
z_{1-\alpha/2}
$$

for $\mu$ gives the large-sample confidence interval

::: {#thm-large-sample-mean-ci}
## Large-Sample Confidence Interval for a Mean

Suppose that $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$ is a random sample from any distribution with finite mean $\mu$ and finite variance $\sigma^2$. If $n$ is large and both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are unknown, an approximate $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is

$$
\boxed{
\overline{X}
\pm
z_{1-\alpha/2}\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}
}
$$
:::

## Why Small Samples Require a Different Distribution {#sec-small-sample-problem}

Suppose that $X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n$ is a random sample from an arbitrary distribution with mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$, where

- $n$ is small, using $n\leq 30$ as a rule of thumb
- both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are unknown

In general, the Central Limit Theorem approximation may not be sufficiently accurate. Therefore, the preceding normal-based interval is not justified merely by replacing $\sigma$ with $S$

Now impose the stronger assumption

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)
$$

Then

$$
\overline{X}
\sim
\mathcal{N}\!\left(\mu,\frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)
$$

but $\sigma^2$, and hence the standard error $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$, remain unknown. For a small sample, simply replacing $\sigma$ with $S$ does not produce a standard normal random variable

## The Student t Pivot {#sec-student-t-pivot}

::: {#def-student-t-distribution}
## Student t Distribution

Let

$$
Z\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
W\sim\chi^2_{\nu}
$$

where $Z$ and $W$ are independent. Then

$$
T
=
\frac{Z}{\sqrt{W/\nu}}
\sim
t_{\nu}
$$

The parameter $\nu$ is called the number of degrees of freedom
:::

For a random sample from a normal distribution,

$$
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
\sim
\mathcal{N}(0,1)
$$

and

$$
\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2}
\sim
\chi^2_{n-1}
$$

These two random variables are independent. Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}
&=
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
\cdot
\frac{\sigma}{S} \\
&=
\frac{
\displaystyle \frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
}{
\displaystyle \sqrt{\frac{S^2}{\sigma^2}}
} \\
&=
\frac{
\underbrace{
\displaystyle \frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}
}_{\color{blue}{\displaystyle Z\sim\mathcal{N}(0,1)}}
}{
\displaystyle
\sqrt{
\frac{
\underbrace{
\displaystyle \frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2}
}_{\color{red}{\displaystyle W\sim\chi^2_{n-1}}}
}{n-1}
}
} \\
&=
\frac{Z}{\sqrt{W/(n-1)}} \\
&\sim
t_{n-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus, when the observations are normally distributed,

$$
\boxed{
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}
\sim
t_{n-1}
}
$$

## t Critical Values {#sec-t-critical-values}

Let $t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}$ denote the $(1-\alpha/2)$ quantile of a Student $t$ distribution with $\nu$ degrees of freedom:

$$
P\!\left(
T_{\nu}
\leq
t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}
\right)
=
1-\frac{\alpha}{2}
$$

By symmetry,

$$
P\!\left(
-t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}
<
T_{\nu}
<
t_{1-\alpha/2,\nu}
\right)
=
1-\alpha
$$

For the statistic

$$
T
=
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}
\sim
t_{n-1}
$$

we therefore have

$$
-t_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}
<
\frac{\overline{X}-\mu}{S/\sqrt{n}}
<
t_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}
$$

Solving for $\mu$ gives the confidence interval

::: {#thm-small-sample-mean-ci}
## Exact Confidence Interval for a Normal Mean

Suppose that

$$
X_1,X_2,\ldots,X_n
\overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim}
\mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)
$$

and both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ are unknown. An exact $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $\mu$ is

$$
\boxed{
\overline{X}
\pm
t_{1-\alpha/2,n-1}\frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}
}
$$
:::

## Example: Sensor Lifetime {#sec-example-sensor-lifetime}

::: {#exm-sensor-lifetime}
## Confidence Interval for Mean Sensor Lifetime

A small study is conducted to test a new sensor for a continuous glucose monitoring system. Previous studies suggest that sensor lifetime, measured in days, is normally distributed

A random sample of $20$ patients is fitted with the new sensor. The observed sample statistics are

$$
n=20
\qquad
\overline{x}=187
\qquad
s^2=16.2
$$

Find a $95\%$ confidence interval for the true mean sensor lifetime $\mu$

For a $95\%$ confidence interval,

$$
\alpha=0.05
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
1-\frac{\alpha}{2}=0.975
$$

The number of degrees of freedom is

$$
\nu=n-1=19
$$

In R, the critical value is obtained with

```{r}
#| eval: false
qt(0.975, df = 19)
```

which gives

$$
t_{0.975,19}=2.093024
$$

Therefore,

$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{x}
\pm
t_{0.975,19}\frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
&=
187
\pm
2.093024
\frac{\sqrt{16.2}}{\sqrt{20}} \\
&\approx
187\pm1.88 \\
&\approx
\boxed{(185.11,188.88)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The $95\%$ confidence interval for the true mean sensor lifetime is approximately

$$
(185.11,188.88)\text{ days}
$$
:::

## Reflection
